1:
The chapter opens not on a battlefield but on a muster-roll, and the witnesses gather to explain the standing army David raised once God had given him rest from his enemies. The reformed John Gill first fixes what is being numbered: not the whole body of the people but the militia of the nation, which, after the courses of priests and Levites, is here divided into twelve monthly courses. Kimchi and Jarchi place the arrangement at the very beginning of David's reign, and the instance of Asahel — slain while David was yet king in Hebron (2 Samuel 2:18) — proves it was framed that early, though it is set down only now.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lay out the architecture of the whole chapter: the organization of the army, the princes of the twelve tribes, the managers of the royal domains, and the king's chief councillors. They bind the muster directly to the census of 1 Chronicles 21, for David, in the last year of his reign, laboured to give a more stable form to the political constitution of the kingdom, and numbered the people precisely in order to perfect this division into alternating monthly service. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, reads the verse as the heading or superscription of the list, the stress lying on "after their number": the "chief fathers" are heads of clans, the "officers" scribes who kept the muster-rolls like recruiting sergeants, and the "courses" — the same Hebrew word used of the Levitical classes in the foregoing chapters — are here military divisions, corps that came in and went out month by month, twenty-four thousand strong, a sort of national guard ever ready for immediate service.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry admires the prudence of the model: David kept twenty-four thousand constantly in arms yet changed them every month, so that the whole militia amounted to two hundred and eighty-eight thousand — perhaps a fifth of the able men — all disciplined, yet none burdened above one month in the year. It is the wisdom of governors, he says, to make the public safety effectual and yet easy. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reckons the same arithmetic — twelve captains, each with a second, over twenty-four thousand apiece, twelve times over an effective force near three hundred thousand, and all "without the smallest expense to the state or the king" — while John Wesley adds that the monthly rotation was contrived so that the burden might fall easy and be equally distributed among the people. Henry alone lifts the muster into a spiritual key: those who are Israelites indeed must learn war, keeping the whole armour of God in readiness, for in the kingdoms of this world readiness for war is itself the security of peace.
2:
The roster proper begins, and the witnesses attend both to the first captain and to the calendar his course opens. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch name him: over the first course, for the first month, was Jashobeam the son of Zabdiel, and to his division were reckoned twenty-four thousand men. They identify him with the hero of 1 Chronicles 11:11 and 2 Samuel 23:8 — the chief of David's mighty men — so that the monthly courses are seen at once to be led not by untried officers but by the champions already celebrated in the roll of the worthies. Charles Ellicott confirms the identification, noting that the names of all twelve generals of division have already occurred in that catalogue of David's heroes, and reads the phrase "in his course" as "upon his course," the commander set over the corps whose turn fell in the opening month.

The reformed John Gill supplies the calendar, glossing "the first month" as Nisan, sometimes called Abib, answering to our March. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke enlarges the point from the Targum, which names each month in turn: the first Nisan, the second Aiyar, then Sivan, Tammuz, Ab, Elul, Tishri, Marchesvan, Cisleu, Tebeth, Shebat, and Adar — and Clarke observes that no mention is made of a Veadar, the intercalary month, so that the scheme runs through a plain twelve-month year without the added month by which the Hebrew calendar was from time to time corrected.

The witnesses do not divide; together they open the list on a note of order and dignity. The first place falls to the first of the heroes, in the first month of the year, over the first of the twelve equal divisions — a symmetry the compiler plainly relishes. What Gill and Clarke add from the calendar and what Keil and Ellicott add from the parallel rolls of 1 Chronicles 11 converge on the same impression: that David's standing army was no rude levy but a carefully articulated institution, each course tied to a named month and each month to a proven commander, the whole turning over like a well-ordered wheel through the seasons of the year.
3:
A single clause about Jashobeam draws the two grammatical-historical witnesses into a knot of interpretation. Keil and Delitzsch note that the verse adds of him that he was of the sons, or descendants, of Perez, and the head of all the army-chiefs in the division for the first month. Charles Ellicott seizes on the pedigree first: Jashobeam belonged to the branch of Judah called Perez, or Pharez, the very branch from which David himself sprang, so that the foremost of the captains shared the blood of the royal house.

The harder phrase is "the chief of all the captains of the host." Ellicott confesses it obscure and weighs three ways of taking it. It may mean that Jashobeam, as the first of David's heroes, enjoyed a kind of precedence among the twelve commanders of the army-corps — though he was not commander-in-chief of the national forces, which office belonged to Joab. Or it may merely emphasise that Jashobeam was "the first" in the rotation of the generals, the phrase "for the first month" explaining what precedes it. Or, again, "the captains of the hosts" may denote the officers of the subdivisions within the first corps, of whom Jashobeam was of course the chief — an explanation Ellicott thinks the context rather favours.

The witnesses do not so much divide as leave the question honestly open. Keil is content to say that Jashobeam was the head of the army-chiefs for his month; Ellicott lays out the alternatives and inclines gently toward the last. What both refuse is to make of the phrase a claim that would trespass on Joab's known place as general of all the host; whatever "chief of all the captains" means, it is a precedence among the twelve, not a supremacy over the kingdom's whole army. The small difficulty is itself instructive, showing how carefully the compiler distinguished ranks — the one commander-in-chief, the twelve monthly generals, and beneath them the officers of thousands and hundreds — in an army whose very orderliness has left its traces even in the obscurities of the record. That the head of this graded host should be a son of Perez, a kinsman of David, only knits the military order the more closely to the throne it was framed to serve.
4:
The second month's course brings a textual tangle, and the two witnesses labour over a corrupt and obscure line. Keil and Delitzsch observe that before the name Dodai something has dropped out: according to 1 Chronicles 11:12 the words "the son of Eleazar" have fallen away, so that the true captain is Eleazar the son of Dodai the Ahohite. Charles Ellicott presses the same correction, holding it proved by that parallel that the right reading is Eliezer son of Dodai the Ahohite, the general set over the corps of the second month with its twenty-four thousand men.

The graver puzzle is the clause about Mikloth. The Hebrew, rendered woodenly, runs "and his course, and Mikloth the ruler," which Ellicott calls all but meaningless; he suspects the "and" before Mikloth is spurious, comparing the similar broken words at the end of the sixth verse. Keil and Delitzsch report the conjecture of Bertheau, who would strike the same letter and read "and his division, Mikloth the prince," taking it to mean that this was the division over which Mikloth presided under the Ahohite. Older commentators, they note, had already understood Mikloth to be chief of the division under Eleazar; and the one thing certain is that Mikloth is a genuine name, occurring among the Benjamites at 1 Chronicles 8:32 and 1 Chronicles 9:37.

The witnesses agree, then, on the shape of the difficulty even where the cure is uncertain. Both trace the confusion to a defective text, both mend the captain's name from the parallel list of the mighty men, and both leave the role of Mikloth in a modest obscurity — a second officer, or a chief within the corps, whose exact place the damaged words no longer let us fix. Ellicott adds a note on the versions that quietly measures the problem: the Septuagint and Vulgate agree with the received English here, while the Syriac and Arabic are altogether wanting in this chapter, so that the ancient witnesses give little help toward the cure. It is a small but honest reminder that even a bare military register has come down worn at the edges, and that the grammatical-historical labour is often less a matter of grand debate than of patiently reading a broken line by the light of its parallels.
5:
The third month's captain, Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, occasions the chapter's sharpest verbal debate, for the text seems to call this warrior "a chief priest." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch resolve it by grammar: the word "head" does not belong to "the priest" but is the predicate of Benaiah himself — Benaiah, son of Jehoiada, as head, that is, of the division for the third month — and they recall that this is the Benaiah who was a hero among the thirty and set over them (1 Chronicles 11:22; 2 Samuel 23:20). Charles Ellicott reads it much the same way: better "son of Jehoiada the priest, as head" — the priesthood being his father's, the headship his own — though he grants the word may be an erroneous gloss, and notes that both the Septuagint and Vulgate go the other way and make Benaiah himself the priest.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke cuts the knot with a lexical stroke. Why not read simply "Benaiah, the son of Jehoiada the priest, a captain"? The Hebrew word rendered "priest" — kohen — signifies both priest and prince, and here, he insists, it should be a principal officer or chief ruler; for we well know that Benaiah was never a priest. Clarke distinguishes him carefully from Benaiah the Pirathonite who commanded the eleventh course, and appeals to the Targum, which styles this Benaiah simply a chief.

So the witnesses circle one question three ways. Is Benaiah called "a chief priest," the title of his father Jehoiada carried over to him? Is the priesthood strictly Jehoiada's, and Benaiah only the "head" of his corps? Or does kohen here bear its wider sense of prince, so that no priesthood is meant at all? Keil and Ellicott lodge the priesthood with the father and the headship with the son; Clarke removes the priestly sense entirely, reading kohen as ruler; and the old versions, as Ellicott reports, boldly make the soldier a priest. None of them will have the plain warrior Benaiah turned into a ministering priest against the plain facts of his life; the disagreement is only over which reading of a single ambiguous word best saves the appearances. The debate, small as it is, shows the witnesses' shared instinct: where a word may mislead, let grammar, the versions, and the known history of the man together decide, rather than the surface of the English rendering.
6:
The verse dwells a moment longer on Benaiah before passing to the next commander, and the two witnesses read its praise and its difficulty together. Keil and Delitzsch render it plainly: this is that Benaiah who was mighty among the thirty and above the thirty, and in his course was Ammizabad his son. They set the honour in its context — Benaiah held not only the command of the third monthly corps but a place among David's elite, more honoured than the thirty of whom he was reckoned one (1 Chronicles 11:25; 2 Samuel 23:23).

Charles Ellicott presses the literal Hebrew — "he, Benaiah, was a hero of the thirty" — and then turns to the clause about the son. "In his course was Ammizabad his son": here, as with Mikloth in the fourth verse, the Septuagint and Vulgate give the sense "over his course," as though Ammizabad were coadjutor with his father in the command. Ellicott suspects the text may be defective in both places alike, the two clauses having once run in the same form; the son, that is, stood in his father's division either as helper in the command or as the one who bore its charge in his room.

The witnesses do not divide over the substance. They agree that Benaiah's eminence among the mighty men is the reason his name is lingered over, and that the mention of Ammizabad follows a pattern already met — a son named beside or beneath the father in the leadership of the course. Ellicott's comparison of the two damaged clauses, Mikloth's and Ammizabad's, is itself a quiet argument: the very repetition of the difficulty suggests an original formula, worn alike in each place, in which the son's relation to the division was more exactly stated than the present text allows. What survives clearly is the honour — Benaiah mighty among the thirty and above them — and the succession, the father's corps passing in some sense to the son. The grammatical-historical labour here, as so often in this register, is content to mark the eminence, note the parallel corruption, and leave the exact office of the son where the text has left it, in a shadow that the parallels light but do not wholly dispel.
7:
The fourth captain is Asahel the brother of Joab, and his name raises at once the question of date, for Asahel was long dead before any such standing army could be marshalled. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws the chronological inference boldly: this verse proves that the division and arrangement were made before David was acknowledged king in Hebron, since Asahel, the fourth captain, was slain by Abner while Ishbosheth still reigned over Israel at Mahanaim (2 Samuel 2:18).

The grammatical-historical witnesses read the same fact from the added clause. Charles Ellicott notes that from this point the record falls into an abridged form — "the fourth, for the fourth month" — and that the appended words "and Zebadiah his son after him" plainly refer to Asahel's early death: the fourth division may have been called by the name of the fallen hero in honour of his memory, as Bertheau suggests, while his son Zebadiah actually held the command. Keil and Delitzsch say the same in stronger terms: Asahel was slain by Abner (2 Samuel 2:18) in the beginning of David's reign, long before the division here recorded, so that his name stands at the head of the course only honoris causa, the corps being named after the dead father though led by the living son.

The witnesses thus converge on a single, telling conclusion. The presence of Asahel among the twelve is not an anachronism to be explained away but a clue to the antiquity of the whole scheme: the framework of the monthly courses reached back to David's earliest days, when Asahel still lived, and was afterward revived and left in order for the peaceable reign of Solomon. Ellicott even wonders whether the difficult clauses about Mikloth and Ammizabad in the earlier verses were once framed exactly like this one about Zebadiah — a son named as successor to a father — so that the naming of the dead Asahel becomes a key to reading the worn places before it. What the fallen brother of Joab supplies, in short, is a date: a course of the army that bears his name must have been ordered while he yet drew breath, and the honour paid to his memory has fixed, for the careful reader, the early origin of David's militia.
8:
The fifth month's captain, Shamhuth, sets the two witnesses to reconciling his name across the parallel lists and tracing his family. Keil and Delitzsch note that the man called Shamhuth here appears as Shammoth at 1 Chronicles 11:27 and as Shammah at 2 Samuel 23:25; he was born at Harod, and the title given him here, "the Izrahite," they take to be equivalent to "the Zarhite" — a member of the family of Zerah the son of Judah (1 Chronicles 2:4).

Charles Ellicott reads the verse in the same abridged form — "the fifth, for the fifth month, was the captain Shamhuth" — and agrees on the man's several names, calling him "Shammoth the Harorite" in the one place and "Shammah the Harodite" in the other. He too suspects "the Izrahite" a mere slip for "the Zarhite," pointing forward to the like designation of the captains of the eighth and tenth months, and fixing Harod as the warrior's native town. So both witnesses, working the same evidence, arrive at one portrait: a mighty man of the family of Zerah, of the town of Harod, whose name the copyists spelled now one way and now another.

The convergence is itself the lesson of the verse. In a register so bare — a name, a month, and the recurring twenty-four thousand — the grammatical-historical labour is chiefly to hold the parallel rolls of David's heroes steadily side by side, so that Shamhuth, Shammoth, and Shammah are seen to be one man, and the varying gentilic resolved to its true form. The thread of Zerah, which Keil and Ellicott catch here, will run on through the roster, binding several of the monthly captains to a single clan of Judah; and the very fact that so obscure a soldier can be traced by his family and his birthplace testifies to the care with which the compiler drew his list from the older records. Where the untrained eye sees only a strange name and a number, the witnesses recover a man of known lineage and known town, one of the champions of the kingdom set over the fifth of David's twelve equal courses.
9:
The sixth month's course falls to Ira the son of Ikkesh the Tekoite, and here the single witness at hand says only enough to place the man. Keil and Delitzsch identify him with the mighty man of the same name and style in the parallel rolls, referring the reader to 1 Chronicles 11:28 and 2 Samuel 23:26, where Ira son of Ikkesh of Tekoa stands among David's heroes. To his corps, like every other, were reckoned twenty-four thousand men, ready for the sixth month of the year.

Sparse as the notice is, the manner of it is significant. The grammatical-historical method here does not moralise or expound; it anchors. By tying Ira of the monthly roster to Ira of the roll of the worthies, Keil and Delitzsch quietly make the same point that runs through the whole list: the twelve commanders were not new men lifted up for this administrative purpose, but the proven champions of David's wars, each already enrolled among the mighty. Tekoa, the man's town, is the same hill-country of Judah that would later give the prophet Amos his home and the wise woman her errand to David; the compiler's bare "the Tekoite" fixes Ira to a real place on the map of Judah.

What the witness leaves unsaid is as telling as what he says. There is no exploit here, no anecdote, only a name, a patronymic, a town, and a number — and yet the reader who follows Keil's cross-references back to the earlier chapter finds the exploit waiting there, in the catalogue of the thirty. The economy is the compiler's own: having once celebrated these men for their deeds, he now enrols them for their office, trusting the earlier record to supply what the roster omits. So the sixth course, headed by Ira the Tekoite, takes its place in the turning year exactly as its fellows do, a division of twenty-four thousand under a captain whose glory is written not here but in the roll of David's mighty men, to which the witness points. The very plainness of the verse is a kind of testimony to the settled, unremarkable order of an army that had learned to run by months and courses under commanders whose names needed no retelling.
10:
The seventh course is led by Helez the Pelonite, and the lone witness marks chiefly his tribe. Keil and Delitzsch refer him to the parallel notices at 1 Chronicles 11:27 and 2 Samuel 23:26, and observe what the verse itself adds — that Helez was "of the children of Ephraim." His division, like the rest, numbered twenty-four thousand for the seventh month of the year.

The tribal note is worth the pause the witness gives it. Most of the twelve captains, as the roster will show, were men of Judah, the royal tribe; but here, in the seventh month, the command falls to an Ephraimite, and later, in the eleventh, to another. The compiler's care to record the tribe of each general quietly testifies that David's standing army was drawn from the whole house of Israel and not from his own tribe alone — that the northern tribes as well as the southern furnished commanders to the monthly courses. Keil and Delitzsch, by pointing back to the roll of the mighty men, again fasten this administrative office to a proven warrior: the Helez set over the seventh corps is the same Helez whose valour had already earned him a place among David's heroes.

There is a further, silent grace in the arrangement the witness lets us see. That Ephraim — the tribe whose jealousy of Judah would one day tear the kingdom in two — should here hold command in David's army beside the men of Judah, and on equal terms, one month in twelve, shows the united Israel that David's ordering of the realm was meant to serve. The bare gentilic "of the children of Ephraim" is thus no idle scrap of genealogy; in the hands of a witness who reads the whole list together, it becomes a token of the breadth of the king's rule, gathering Ephraim and Judah, north and south, into one disciplined host. The seventh month's twenty-four thousand stand under an Ephraimite captain, and the kingdom is the more firmly knit for it — a small but real witness to the concord David laboured to build and to leave, in arms as in worship, to his son.
11:
The eighth month's captain is Sibbecai the Hushathite, and the single witness places him by his parallels and his clan. Keil and Delitzsch send the reader to 1 Chronicles 11:29 and 2 Samuel 23:27, where Sibbecai stands among the mighty men, and they note that he was "of the Zarhites" — of the family of Zerah, the same house of Judah to which the fifth month's Shamhuth and the tenth month's Maharai belonged. His corps, too, counted twenty-four thousand for its month.

The recurrence of the Zarhite name is the thread the witness quietly draws. Zerah, the twin of Pharez and son of Judah, was not the branch through which the royal line ran; that honour fell to Pharez, David's own ancestor. Yet Zerah's house, passed over for the throne, is here seen furnishing captain after captain to David's army — Shamhuth, Sibbecai, Maharai — so that the elder twin's line, though it did not give Israel its king, gave it a good share of its commanders. Keil and Delitzsch, by marking each of these men a Zarhite, let the reader feel how the whole tribe of Judah, in its several branches, stood behind the throne it had produced.

Sibbecai is numbered elsewhere among the champions who withstood the Philistines, and the witness's cross-reference to the roll of the worthies keeps that proven valour in view even where the roster is silent. The pattern is by now familiar: a name, a gentilic, a number, and behind them a man whose exploits are written in the earlier chapters. What the eighth verse of the list adds to the whole is one more Zarhite, one more proven hero set over one more equal division of the host. The grammatical-historical reading finds its reward precisely in such connexions — in seeing that the bare register of monthly courses is woven, name by name, into the larger fabric of Judah's genealogy and David's wars, so that even the least-noticed captain is anchored both to a family in the tribal rolls and to a deed in the story of the kingdom. The Hushathite Sibbecai, of the house of Zerah, takes his ordered place among the twelve, and the line of the rejected twin is honoured in him.
12:
The ninth course is given to Abiezer the Anetothite, and the witness fixes his town and tribe. Keil and Delitzsch cite the parallels at 1 Chronicles 11:28 and 2 Samuel 23:27, and identify Anathoth as the place in the tribe of Benjamin (Jeremiah 1:1) from which he came — the same priestly town that would one day be the home of the prophet Jeremiah. His division numbered twenty-four thousand for the ninth month.

Here the roster turns from Judah and Ephraim to Benjamin, and the witness's small geographical note carries more than it seems. Anathoth was a city of the priests, set apart within Benjamin's border, and the captain of the ninth month is marked as coming from it. The compiler's practice of naming each general's town roots the whole army in the actual soil of Israel — this course under a man of Anathoth, that under a man of Harod or Tekoa or Netophah — so that the twelve divisions are not abstractions but bodies of men gathered under captains of known homes, drawn from the length and breadth of the land.

Keil and Delitzsch's habit of anchoring each name to the roll of the mighty men holds good again: the Abiezer of the ninth corps is the Abiezer already enrolled among David's heroes. And the mention of Anathoth, which the witness ties to the opening of Jeremiah's book, casts a long shadow forward; the town that here supplies a loyal captain to David would centuries later be the birthplace of the weeping prophet, and the scene of the plot against his life. The genealogist of the army did not know this, but the reader who follows the cross-reference cannot help feeling the reach of these obscure names down the corridors of later Scripture. For the present it is enough that Benjamin, Saul's own tribe, furnishes a commander to David's host, and that the ninth month's twenty-four thousand stand under a man of the priestly city — one more sign of the wide and settled order by which David bound the tribes together in the service of the crown.
13:
The tenth month's captain is Maharai the Netophathite, and the single witness marks his family and his fellows. Keil and Delitzsch refer him to 1 Chronicles 11:30 and 2 Samuel 23:28, and note that he too belonged to the family of Zerah — a Zarhite, like the captains of the fifth and eighth months before him. His corps counted its twenty-four thousand for the tenth month of the year.

With Maharai the thread of Zerah shows itself a third time, and the witness's care to record it lets the reader see a pattern the roster would otherwise hide. Three of the twelve commanders — Shamhuth, Sibbecai, and now Maharai — spring from the one house of Zerah, the twin passed over when the sceptre went to Pharez. The line that did not carry the throne carries, in these men, a full quarter of the monthly generalship; and the compiler, by fixing each of them as a Zarhite, quietly honours the elder branch of Judah in the very register of David's Pharez-descended kingdom.

Maharai's town, Netophah, lay near Bethlehem, David's own city, in the heart of Judah; and the gentilic "the Netophathite" places him, like his fellow-captains, in a real and known locality. The grammatical-historical labour, here as throughout the roster, is content to do three quiet things: to identify the man with his namesake in the roll of the mighty men, to fix his clan within the tribe, and to note his home. From these the larger picture assembles itself — an army officered by the proven heroes of Judah and Israel, its commanders traceable by family and by town, its twelve courses turning through the months under men whose names the earlier chapters had already crowned with honour. The tenth course, under Maharai the Zarhite of Netophah, is one more panel in that orderly design, and the recurrence of Zerah's name within it is the witness's reminder that in the economy of the kingdom the rejected line was not forgotten, but given its own honourable place in the defence of the throne that had passed its elder brother by.
14:
The eleventh course belongs to Benaiah the Pirathonite, and the witness is careful to distinguish him and to name his tribe. Keil and Delitzsch send the reader to 1 Chronicles 11:31 and 2 Samuel 23:30, where this Benaiah of Pirathon stands among the mighty men; and the verse itself adds that he was "of the children of Ephraim." His division, like all the rest, mustered twenty-four thousand for its month.

This is the second Benaiah of the roster, and the second Ephraimite. He must not be confused with the famous Benaiah the son of Jehoiada who led the third course and stood chief over the thirty; this is another man, of Pirathon in the hill-country of Ephraim, and the witness's cross-reference to the roll of the worthies keeps the two apart while honouring both. That Ephraim should again furnish a commander — as it did for the seventh month — underscores what the earlier tribal notes had begun to show: the northern tribes held their share of the generalship, and the eleventh month's twenty-four thousand stood under an Ephraimite as the seventh month's had done.

The plainness of the notice is by now the roster's settled manner. Keil and Delitzsch neither expound nor moralise; they identify, and by identifying they connect. The Benaiah of the eleventh corps is fastened to the Benaiah of the mighty men, his town of Pirathon fixed in Ephraim, his course numbered like its fellows. And yet the accumulation of such bare notices has its own eloquence: verse upon verse, the list builds an army in which every commander is a known hero of a known town, drawn from Judah and Benjamin and Ephraim alike, each set over an equal division, each answerable for a single month. The witness's discipline — always the same three strokes of parallel, tribe, and number — is what lets the whole design stand clear. The eleventh course, under Benaiah of Pirathon of Ephraim, carries the roster nearly to its close, one more proven man in one more ordered place, the great turning wheel of David's militia now within a month of completing its round.
15:
The twelfth and last course completes the year under Heldai the Netophathite, and the two witnesses close the roster by settling his name and then surveying the whole. Keil and Delitzsch note that Heldai appears as Heled at 1 Chronicles 11:30 and, by an evident error, as Heleb at 2 Samuel 23:29; he belonged to the family of Othniel (Joshua 15:17). Charles Ellicott glosses the name — Heldai, "living," the same as Heled, "life" — fixes his clan as that of Othniel and his town as Netophah near Bethlehem, and his corps, like all before it, held its twenty-four thousand for the twelfth month.

Having reached the end, Ellicott pauses to count the tribes of the twelve generals, and his tally is the chapter's own comment on itself. Eight of the twelve — the captains of the first, third, fourth, fifth, sixth, eighth, tenth, and twelfth months — belonged to the royal tribe of Judah. Of the remaining four, the second was perhaps and the ninth certainly a Benjamite, while the seventh and the eleventh were Ephraimites. The great preponderance of Judah is thus plain: two-thirds of the monthly command lay with David's own tribe, the rest divided between Benjamin and Ephraim.

The witnesses do not divide, and Ellicott's summary gathers up what the single tribal notes had been quietly telling all along. David's standing army was officered chiefly, but not solely, from Judah; Benjamin, the tribe of Saul, and Ephraim, the proud northern house, each held their courses too, so that the twelve months turned under a leadership drawn from the heart and from the borders of the nation together. Keil's patient correction of Heldai's name from the parallel rolls, and Ellicott's closing census of the captains' tribes, are of a piece: the one shows the care with which each man was identified, the other the balance with which the whole was composed. So the roster ends as it began, in order and proportion — the last of the proven heroes set over the last of the equal divisions, the year's full round of two hundred and eighty-eight thousand accounted for, and the reader left to see, in Ellicott's arithmetic, both the primacy of Judah and the breadth of an army that reached beyond it.
16:
With the army numbered, the record turns to the princes of the tribes, and the witnesses first explain how these rulers differ from the captains just named. The reformed John Gill draws the distinction sharply: the monthly captains were of David's own appointment, but these princes ruled over their respective tribes in their own right, or by their tribes' choice — Eliezer the son of Zichri over Reuben, Shephatiah the son of Maachah over Simeon, and so down the list, with Zadok, soon to be high priest under Solomon, set over the Aaronites as distinct from the Levites. Charles Ellicott adds the constitutional history: the emir of each tribe had once been its leader in war as well as its chief in peace, but David, as the roster of the courses shows, had taken the war-leadership to himself and to his own nominated commanders, leaving the tribal princes to manage the internal affairs of their people.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that the enumeration follows hard upon the army-list because the two belong together — the chief management of the people's internal business, tribe by tribe, lay in these princes' hands. They observe the curious gap: Gad and Asher are omitted, as Dan and Zebulun had been in the genealogies of 1 Chronicles 4 through 7; and Levi is represented twice, once by its own prince and once by Zadok the head of the Aaronite priesthood. Here a small debate opens. Keil confesses the omission of Gad and Asher to be "for reasons unknown to us," while the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supposes rather that the account of these two tribes has simply been lost from the register, their rulers, like the lords-lieutenant of a county, being honorary men without pay.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the whole passage, lifts the bare list into reflection. Something of the ancient order Moses set up survived here, that every tribe should have its chief; Elihu, prince of Judah, was David's own eldest brother, descended in a straight line from Nahshon. Even Jaaziel the son of Abner held a place, though Abner had been David's enemy — a preferment Henry reads as rendering good for evil. And looking ahead to the numbering and the officers of the court, Henry finds a moral in each: the census David took in the pride of his heart "turned to no good account," and the king's stewards were all men "for service," not for state or sport, so that David, with all these counsellors about him, still preferred his Bible before them all, saying that God's testimonies were his delight and his counsellors (Psalms 119:24). The witnesses thus give the verse both its plain sense — the tribal princes, ruling by right beneath the king — and its larger frame, the settled polity of a kingdom ordered from the throne down to the tribe.
17:
The list of princes continues with the tribe of Levi, and the two witnesses note the double representation it receives. Keil and Delitzsch remark that for Levi, unlike the other tribes, two heads are named: over the Levites, Hashabiah the son of Kemuel, and over the Aaronites, Zadok — the latter being the Nagid of Aaron, the head of the priesthood, Zadok the high priest of the family of Eleazar. Charles Ellicott renders the Hebrew idiom exactly: "to Levi, Hashabiah; to Aaron, Zadok," and confirms that Zadok is the high priest of 1 Chronicles 24:3, so that Levi has one prince for the tribe at large and one for its great Aaronite branch.

The witnesses agree that this doubling is no accident but a fitting recognition of Levi's peculiar place. Alone among the tribes, Levi was set apart for the sanctuary, and within Levi the sons of Aaron were further set apart for the priesthood proper; the civil ordering of the tribe therefore required two heads, one for the whole body of Levites and one for the priestly house that stood at its heart. That the Aaronite prince should be Zadok — soon, under Solomon, to hold the high priesthood alone — gives the notice a quiet weight, for it names the man in whom the priestly line would be firmly established.

The grammatical-historical reading here is chiefly a matter of construction and identification, and both witnesses do the same work. Ellicott's careful "to Levi... to Aaron" catches the structure the English blurs; Keil's identification of Zadok as head of the family of Eleazar fixes the man within the priestly genealogy. Together they show that David's ordering of the tribes did not overlook the peculiar constitution of Levi, but honoured it with a twofold headship, the tribe and its priesthood each having its prince. So even in this bare enumeration the special dignity of Levi is preserved: where the secular tribes have each a single ruler, the tribe of the altar has two, and the name of Zadok, standing over the Aaronites, points forward to the settled priesthood of the temple age. The witnesses leave the verse as a small monument to order — every tribe under its head, and the holy tribe under two, according to the double sanctity that marked it out from all the rest.
18:
The princes of Judah and Issachar bring the two witnesses to a point of identification and a curious historical echo. Keil and Delitzsch note that "Elihu, one of the brethren of David," set over Judah, is only another form of the name Eliab (1 Chronicles 2:13), David's eldest brother, who as Jesse's firstborn had become the tribal prince of Judah. Charles Ellicott agrees, observing that the Septuagint indeed reads "Eliab" here, and that Eliab, as the eldest son of Jesse, held the princedom by right of the firstborn, the house of Jesse being the leading family of Judah (Ruth 4:17).

That the prince of Judah should be David's own eldest brother is a detail the witnesses let speak for itself. The king had been the youngest of Jesse's sons, passed over and sent to the sheep while his elders stood before Samuel; now the eldest of those elders holds the civil headship of the tribe, while the youngest wears the crown. The arrangement honours the ancient right of the firstborn within the tribe even as the throne rests on the divine choice of the last-born — a quiet coexistence of custom and election that the compiler records without comment.

Over Issachar Ellicott sets Omri the son of Michael, and here he catches a longer echo: this Omri was perhaps an ancestor of the more famous adventurer of the same name who would one day seize the northern throne and found the dynasty of Ahab (1 Kings 16:16; Micah 6:16). The suggestion is offered lightly, a possible thread between a loyal prince of David's Israel and the house that would later trouble it. The witnesses, working the same grammatical-historical craft, thus draw from two names both a backward and a forward look — Elihu the eldest brother binding this list to the genealogy of Jesse, and Omri of Issachar perhaps binding it, by blood, to the kings of Samaria yet to come. Neither point is pressed into doctrine; both are the fruit of reading the register against the whole sweep of the sacred history, in which even the princes of David's tribes are found to stand in the long lines that run back to the patriarchs and forward to the divided kingdom.
19:
The princes of Zebulun and Naphtali receive only a bare mention, and the single witness passes over them with little more than their names. Keil and Delitzsch record that over Zebulun was set Ishmaiah the son of Obadiah, and over Naphtali Jerimoth the son of Azriel — two of the northern tribes taking their place in the roll of rulers between the Ephraimite and Manassite princes named on either side.

Yet the very inclusion of Zebulun is worth a moment's notice, for the witness elsewhere marks that this tribe had been omitted from the earlier genealogies of 1 Chronicles 4 and following; here, in the list of David's princes, it is present and provided with a head. Whatever gaps the tribal registers may show, the roll of the kingdom's rulers reaches out to Zebulun and Naphtali alike, the far northern tribes by the Galilean hills, and gives each its prince under the crown. The compiler's evident aim is completeness of governance: as many of the tribes as can be named are furnished with their chief, so that the whole house of Israel, north as well as south, is seen to be ordered under David's rule.

The plainness of the notice is itself characteristic of this section of the chapter. Keil and Delitzsch, who elsewhere untangle broken clauses and disputed names, here have nothing to add but the names themselves, for the record gives nothing more; a prince for Zebulun, a prince for Naphtali, and no exploit or difficulty attached to either. The grammatical-historical reading respects that silence, neither inventing significance nor passing the verse by, but setting Ishmaiah and Jerimoth in their proper place in the design — two more of the tribal heads through whom the internal affairs of Israel were managed while the king and his captains kept the field. In an ordered kingdom even the obscurest office is filled and recorded, and the witness is content to let these two northern princes stand quietly in the roll, their names preserved though their deeds are not, as tokens of the reach of David's government to the utmost borders of the land.
20:
The princes of Ephraim and of western Manasseh bring the two witnesses to a point of geography. Keil and Delitzsch name Hoshea the son of Azaziah over the children of Ephraim, and Joel the son of Pedaiah over the half-tribe of Manasseh; and they explain why Manasseh has two princes in this list — because the one half of the tribe had received its inheritance on this side Jordan and the other beyond it, so that each half required its own head. Charles Ellicott specifies which half is meant here: the half of Manasseh on the west of Jordan, lying between Ephraim and Issachar.

The doubling of Manasseh's headship, like the doubling of Levi's, shows the compiler fitting his register to the real divisions of the nation. Manasseh alone among the tribes was split by the Jordan, one portion settled in the western hills and one in the eastern uplands of Gilead and Bashan; and the roll of princes honours that division by naming a ruler for each half, the western in this verse and the eastern, toward Gilead, in the next. The witnesses read the arrangement as a plain matter of administration — a people divided by a river must be governed in two, and the record faithfully provides for both.

The grammatical-historical labour here is chiefly to mark that geographical point and to keep the two half-tribes distinct. Ellicott's brief note — "that on the west of Jordan, between Ephraim and Issachar" — does exactly this, fixing Joel's charge to the western hills, while Keil's explanation of the twofold princedom supplies the reason. Together they let the reader see how thoroughly David's ordering of the tribes answered to the facts of the land: Ephraim under Hoshea, the western Manassites under Joel, and the eastern Manassites still to be named, each portion of the people under its proper prince. The verse adds no exploit and raises no dispute; it simply carries the roll onward through the central tribes, and in the double provision for Manasseh it quietly testifies to the same care that gave Levi two heads — a government shaped to the true shape of the nation, overlooking neither the sanctity that set Levi apart nor the river that cut Manasseh in two.
21:
The list reaches the eastern half of Manasseh and the tribe of Benjamin, and the two witnesses attach to each name a note of interest. Keil and Delitzsch record Iddo the son of Zechariah over the half-tribe of Manasseh in Gilead — the eastern Manassites beyond Jordan — and Jaasiel the son of Abner over Benjamin. Charles Ellicott renders the phrase "toward Gilead, Gilead-ward," fixing this half of the tribe east of the Jordan, in Gilead and Bashan, over against the western half named in the verse before.

Two of the names draw the witnesses' comment. Ellicott observes that the prophet Zechariah was a son of Berechiah, the son of Iddo, and may well have descended from this Iddo the prince of eastern Manasseh — a possible root, running down through the generations, for the voice that would one day call the returned exiles to rebuild the temple. And of Jaasiel he remarks that he was doubtless a son of Abner, Saul's famous marshal — the very Abner who had once striven in arms to keep David from the throne, yet whose son is here found among the honoured princes of the tribes.

The witnesses thus read in two names a backward and a forward reach. Jaasiel binds the list to the old rivalry of the house of Saul, now healed in the honour paid to Abner's son; Iddo perhaps binds it forward to the prophet of the restoration. The grammatical-historical craft that fixes the eastern Manassites to their trans-Jordan seat is the same craft that catches these genealogical echoes, for it reads each prince not as an isolated name but as a link in the long chain of the sacred history. Benjamin, the tribe of Saul, is here governed by the son of Saul's own general, under the king their house had once opposed; and the eastern Manassites of Gilead have their prince as surely as their western brethren, so that the roll gathers in the tribes beyond Jordan no less than those within it. The verse adds, beneath its administrative surface, a quiet witness both to the reconciliations wrought under David and to the deep continuities of Israel's story, in which the son of an old adversary may yet stand among the rulers of the covenant people.
22:
The roll of princes ends with Dan and a set of questions about who is present and who is missing. Keil and Delitzsch name Azareel the son of Jeroham over Dan and close the list — "these were the princes of the tribes of Israel" — noting that Dan and Zebulun, which had been left out of the earlier tribal genealogies, are both included here, while Gad and Asher, present there, are here passed over "for reasons unknown to us." Charles Ellicott makes the same tally and confesses the same ignorance: the total of twelve is reached by counting Manasseh as two and reckoning Joseph as more than one, and as for the absence of Gad and Asher, "why, we cannot say."

Ellicott adds two further observations. The order of the first six names follows that of Genesis 35:23; and Dan, oddly, is placed last. Some have thought this a mark of the chronicler's disapproval of Dan's ancient idolatry (1 Kings 12:29; Judges 18:30; Amos 8:14), but Ellicott judges it more likely that the compiler simply kept the order of his source. On the omission of Gad, the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley ventures the one positive explanation the witnesses offer: the list gives the princes of most of the tribes, not all, and Gad is probably left out because that tribe was joined with the Reubenites under a single prince.

So the closing verse leaves a small debate honestly unsettled. Why are Gad and Asher unlisted? Keil and Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, refuse to guess and own the reason unknown; Wesley proposes that Gad at least was folded in with Reuben under one head, so that its separate prince would be needless. The witnesses agree on the facts — twelve princes named, Dan and Zebulun restored to the roll, Gad and Asher absent, Dan set last — but divide gently over the cause, some content with candid ignorance and one offering a reasoned conjecture. It is a fitting close to the list: an ordered roll of the tribal heads, complete enough to show the whole nation governed under the crown, yet frank about the gaps and irregularities that a faithful copyist would neither smooth away nor pretend to explain.
23:
With the two lists finished, the record pauses to note what David did not do — he took not the number of those from twenty years old and under — and the witnesses read the restraint as a matter of faith and of the census recalled. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott treats the verse as a concluding remark on the foregoing catalogues: the heading had promised "the sons of Israel according to their number," and this note limits that number to those above twenty, the men of military age. The reference, he says, is undoubtedly to the census of 1 Chronicles 21, one main object of which was the very military and political organisation the chapter has just described. David restricted the count to those capable of bearing arms because the LORD had promised to increase Israel like the stars of heaven (Genesis 15:5; Genesis 22:17); to attempt to number the whole would be to evince a distrust of that faithfulness, and perhaps could not in any case succeed.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the motive the same way: to number them all, both above and under twenty, would have been an infinite trouble and a tempting of God — a questioning of the truth of his promise to multiply them beyond counting.

The reformed John Gill, however, complicates the picture with a darker reading drawn from Cornelius Bertram. On this view David did in fact have some under twenty numbered — youths of sixteen, seventeen, eighteen, or nineteen, if they were robust and tall and able to bear arms — and this, contrary to the law of God, was the very sin of the numbering; yet David, ashamed of it, would not enter their sum in his chronicles, that his transgression might not be known. So a real disagreement stands over the verse. Was the exclusion of the young an act of piety, David sparing the promise the affront of a full count, as Ellicott and Wesley hold? Or does the verse rather conceal a sin, David having wrongly numbered the under-aged and then suppressed the record, as Gill supposes after Bertram? The witnesses divide over the very meaning of the reservation — whether it marks the king's reverence for God's word or veils his offence against it — and the difference reaches back to how each reads the fatal census of the earlier chapter, as presumption checked or as presumption committed and then hidden.
24:
The note on the census darkens further as the witnesses take up Joab and the wrath that fell. The reformed John Gill states the plain fact: Joab the son of Zeruiah began to number the people by David's order, but entirely against his own will. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch complete the sentence — he began but finished not, because there fell wrath for it against Israel, and the number was never put in the account of the chronicles of King David — and Charles Ellicott sharpens each clause: "had begun," pointing back to 1 Chronicles 21:6, where Joab omitted to number Levi and Benjamin; "there fell wrath," a phrase that recurs at 2 Chronicles 24:18; and "was not put in the account," literally "the number came not up," was not entered in the official annals of the reign.

Here a difference of emphasis opens over why the count was never recorded. Keil and Gill lay the weight on the wrath and on David's shame: the numbering was sin, judgment followed, and the king would not own or enter a total obtained by transgression. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke offers a more practical account of the same silence — Joab did not return the whole number, either because the plague began before he had finished, or because, having entered on the work with extreme reluctance, he did not choose to give it in, unwilling to tell the king how numerous the people were.

Ellicott, for his part, turns the unrecorded number into a clue about the compiler's sources. That the count "came not up" into the annals shows that the chronicler had those annals before him — "the account of the chronicles of King David" — and drew the lists of this chapter from them, even as he noted that this one figure never made its way in. So the witnesses read the verse on two levels at once. On the surface it records a judgment: a census begun in pride, cut short by wrath, and struck from the royal record. Beneath it, to Ellicott's eye, lies a window on how the book was made — the writer working from official annals, faithfully reporting even what those annals had refused to contain. Whether the number failed of record through the plague's interruption, or Joab's reluctance, or David's penitent shame, all the witnesses agree on the sober upshot: the fruit of that numbering was wrath, and the king was content to let it be forgotten.
25:
The record now turns from princes and numberings to the stewards of the royal estate, and the witnesses read the opening of this list as a portrait of David's personal wealth. The reformed John Gill runs down the officers in order: over the king's treasures — his gold and silver, brought in by levy or tribute — was Azmaveth the son of Adiel; over the storehouses in the fields, cities, villages, and castles was Jehonathan the son of Uzziah; Ezri the son of Chelub looked after the labourers who tilled the ground; Shimei the Ramathite kept the vineyards; Zabdi of Shepham had charge of the wine in press and cellar; and Baalhanan of Gedor was over the olive and sycamore trees. Each domain, Gill shows, had its own appointed overseer.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws from the whole passage an inventory of what a king's substance then consisted in: treasures of gold and silver; goods and grain in castles, cities, villages, and fields; vineyards and their produce; olive-trees and theirs; herds of cattle in their several districts; camels and asses — for, he notes pointedly, they had no horses — and flocks of sheep and goats. It is the wealth of a great landed prince, not of a merchant or a warlord, and Clarke lets the list speak for the simplicity of the age.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fixes the technical terms. The king's treasures, he says, were those of the palace on Zion; the "storehouses" render the same Hebrew word for treasures, but these lay in the fields, the cities, the villages, and the "castles" or towers scattered through the country, comprising all that belonged to David outside the walls of Jerusalem. Jehonathan, over these, was in effect comptroller-general of the revenues from every such source. The witnesses converge on a single impression: David's household was ordered like a great estate, its treasures within the capital and its produce without, each kind of wealth — grain, wine, oil, cattle, camels, asses, and flocks — committed to a named and responsible steward. There is no pomp in the arrangement, only husbandry; the king who had subdued the nations kept his own fields and vineyards under careful management, and the compiler preserves the roll of his stewards as faithfully as the roll of his captains and his princes, that the good order of the kingdom might be seen to reach from the army in the field to the olive-press in the lowland.
26:
The steward of the king's arable land draws a brief but exact comment from the two witnesses. Keil and Delitzsch name Ezri the son of Chelub as set over those who did the work of the field for the tillage of the ground — the labourers, that is, who ploughed and sowed the royal domains — and pass on to the officers of the vineyards and the wine-stores in the verses following, Shimei the Ramathite over the one and Zabdi the Shiphmite over the other. Charles Ellicott sums Ezri's charge in a phrase: he was steward of the arable domains.

The plainness of the office is itself the point the witnesses quietly make. David's wealth lay largely in worked land, and the working of it required an overseer of the labourers no less than the produce required a keeper of the stores. Keil's care to distinguish the man who managed the field-hands from the men who managed the vineyards and the wine shows how minutely the royal husbandry was divided: one officer for the tillage, another for the vines, another for the wine that came of them. The kingdom's economy, on this reading, was no loose accumulation of goods but a graded administration, each stage of production under its own responsible head.

Ellicott's brief gloss — "steward of the arable domains" — and Keil's fuller placing of Ezri among the field-officers together set the verse in its context within the list of stewards. There is no dispute here and no difficulty of text; the grammatical-historical labour is simply to render the office exactly and to mark its place in the sequence. And yet the very ordinariness of the notice carries a lesson the witnesses elsewhere draw out at length: that the greatness of David's kingdom rested not only on its armies and its worship but on the patient management of its fields, and that the compiler thought the name of the man who oversaw the king's ploughmen worth preserving beside the names of his generals and his priests. Ezri the son of Chelub, steward of the tillage, takes his modest place in the record of a realm ordered from the throne down to the furrow, where nothing needful was left without its overseer.
27:
The officers of the vineyards and of the wine draw the two witnesses to distinguish the labour from its fruit. Charles Ellicott identifies Shimei of Ramah in Benjamin (Joshua 18:25) as overseer of the vineyards, and Zabdi — whom he takes to be the Zebadiah of the south-Judean town of Shiphmoth (1 Samuel 30:28) — as set "over that which is in the vineyards for the treasures of wine," that is, over the wine-cellars. The territory of Judah, he adds, was famous as a wine-growing land (Genesis 49:11); it was there the memorable grapes of Eshcol were gathered (Numbers 13:23).

John Wesley catches the same division of office and states its principle. Shimei was over the vineyards — meaning over the workmen and labourers who dressed and tended them — while the next officer was over the fruit of the vineyards, the wine itself; and just so, he notes, one man was set over the labourers in the fields and another over the fruits of the fields laid up in store. The pattern is deliberate: everywhere in the royal husbandry the tending of the crop and the keeping of its produce are committed to separate hands.

The witnesses, though of different schools, read the verse as one. Ellicott's care to fix the towns — Ramah in Benjamin, Shiphmoth in the Judean south — and to recall the fame of Judah's vineyards, joins with Wesley's care to mark the distinction of function, and both illuminate the same well-ordered economy. The king's vines had their overseer and his wine its keeper; the grapes were tended by one officer and the vintage guarded by another; and the compiler recorded each, down to the man's name and native place. It is a small window on the settled prosperity of David's reign, when the hills of Judah bore their famous grapes and the wine was gathered into royal cellars under a responsible steward. The witnesses leave the verse as a token of that abundance and that order together — a kingdom rich enough in wine to need a keeper of its cellars, and governed carefully enough to appoint one, distinct from the man who kept the vines that filled them.
28:
The stewards of the oil and the trees that yielded it bring the two witnesses to matters of botany and place. Keil and Delitzsch set Baalhanan the Gederite over the olive and sycamore trees in the shephelah, the fertile lowland, and Joash over the cellars of oil; the Gederite, they say, was born at Geder, a town of the Judean hill-country (Joshua 15:58). Charles Ellicott enlarges the picture. The sycamores stood in the shephelah, the lowland of Judah between the hills and the sea (Joshua 15:33); the tree, the fig-mulberry, was a beautiful evergreen once abundant in Palestine, as the days of Solomon attest (1 Kings 10:27; 2 Chronicles 1:15), and its small sweet figs were much eaten by the poor (Amos 7:14).

Ellicott lingers too over the steward's name. Baal-hanan means "the Lord bestowed," an older form of the name Jehohanan — the same element, he notes, that appears in the Phoenician Hannibal — so that the very name of the king's forester confesses the giver of the fruitful trees. The "cellars of oil" he renders "stores of oil," the oil being that pressed from the olives (Judges 9:9), and Joash the keeper of those stores as Baalhanan was the keeper of the groves.

The witnesses divide over nothing here; together they fill out the same scene. The royal olive-yards and sycamore-groves lay in the warm lowland of Judah, tended by a man of the hill-country town of Geder, while the oil they yielded was gathered into stores under another steward — again the tending of the trees and the keeping of their produce set in separate hands, as with the fields and the vineyards before. Ellicott's touches of natural history — the evergreen fig-mulberry, its humble figs the food of the poor — and his reading of the name Baal-hanan as a quiet doxology give the bare notice a warmth beyond its administrative surface. The compiler records only that certain men were over the olives, the sycamores, and the oil; the grammatical-historical witnesses recover from those few words a whole landscape of Judah's lowland orchards and the God whose bounty, the steward's very name declares, had bestowed them.
29:
The overseers of the king's cattle carry the two witnesses out to the pastures of the land. Keil and Delitzsch name Shitrai the Sharonite over the herds that fed in Sharon, and Shaphat the son of Adlai over the herds in the valleys, and go on in the same breath to the officers of the camels, the asses, and the smaller cattle that follow. Charles Ellicott fixes the geography: Sharon — "the Level" — was a fertile strip of pasture-land running along the coast of the Mediterranean between Caesarea and Joppa (Song of Solomon 2:1; Isaiah 33:9), while the "valleys" over which Shaphat was set were apparently those of the Judean highlands.

The witnesses read in the pairing of these officers the same careful division that runs through the whole list of stewards. David's herds did not graze in one place but in two very different regions — the rich coastal plain of Sharon and the upland valleys — and each pasture had its own overseer, Shitrai by the sea and Shaphat in the hills. The compiler's precision in naming the ground, and not merely the man, lets the reader see the reach of the royal husbandry across the varied country of Israel, from the Mediterranean flats to the inland dales.

Ellicott's topographical care — the exact bounds of Sharon between Caesarea and Joppa, the note that another reading gives simply "in valleys" — is characteristic of the grammatical-historical craft, which will not let even a herdsman's district pass unlocated. Keil, for his part, sets these two officers at the head of the pastoral stewards, whose full number, with the keepers of camels, asses, and flocks, he will presently reckon. Together the witnesses show David's cattle distributed over the best grazing of the land and committed, region by region, to responsible hands. There is no dispute and no difficulty here, only the same impression the list has built from the first: a kingdom whose wealth in living herds, as in grain and wine and oil, was ordered under named overseers, each answerable for his own pasture, so that nothing of the king's substance — not the oxen of the coast nor the herds of the valleys — went untended or unrecorded in the careful economy of David's reign.
30:
The keepers of the camels and the asses draw from the single witness a note on names and on the fitness of the men to their charge. Charles Ellicott observes that Obil, set over the camels, bears a name that means "owner of camels" or "a good manager of camels," answering exactly to the Arabic word for such a keeper; and Obil was an Ishmaelite — an Arab, that is, and so the fittest of men to look after camels (Genesis 37:25; Judges 7:12). Over the asses — the she-asses (Genesis 49:14; Zechariah 9:9) — was Jehdeiah the Meronothite, of Meronoth, a town perhaps near Mizpah (Nehemiah 3:7).

The witness makes of Obil's name and race a small but telling observation. That the man over the king's camels should be both named "camel-keeper" and born an Ishmaelite is no idle coincidence; it shows an administration that matched the officer to the office, entrusting the desert beast to a son of the desert who knew its ways. The point is a quiet one, but it opens a window on the character of David's household economy, in which the choice of a steward answered to the nature of his charge.

The grammatical-historical labour here is entirely Ellicott's, and it is a labour of exact detail: the meaning of Obil's name traced to its Arabic root, the "asses" specified as she-asses from the parallel usages of Scripture, the obscure Meronoth located as best it may be near Mizpah. From these particulars the reader gathers the same lesson the whole list teaches, now sharpened to a single case. David's camels, fittest for the deserts on the kingdom's margins, were kept by an Arab of Ishmael's stock; his she-asses by a man of a Benjamite town; and the compiler recorded each keeper by name and origin. It is the meritocracy of an ordered realm, where a man's place was suited to his skill and his people, and where even the overseer of the asses was thought worth a line in the register. The witness leaves the verse as a miniature of the whole — the king's living wealth parcelled out among stewards chosen, name and nation, for the work they were best fitted to do.
31:
The last of the pastoral stewards and the summing of the whole draw all three witnesses. Charles Ellicott names Jaziz the Hagerite over the flocks of sheep and goats, and recalls that the Hagrim, or Hagerites, had been conquered in East Gilead by the tribe of Reuben in the days of Saul (1 Chronicles 5:10); David's flocks and camel-herds, he suggests, may have grazed in the pastures east of Jordan under these Bedawi overseers. The concluding phrase — "all these were the rulers of the substance which was king David's" — he renders with care: "rulers" is the same word elsewhere translated "captains" or "princes," and "substance" is an old term for the movable wealth of a nomad chief (Genesis 12:5), though David's riches now embraced husbandry and commerce too (1 Chronicles 22:4), the age of the kings having exchanged the purely nomad life for an ordered, settled state.

Keil and Delitzsch confirm the reckoning, closing the list of overseers with Jaziz the Hagerite and turning next to the king's councillors. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the moral the summary invites: among all these officers, he observes, there are none for state, none for sport, no master of the ceremonies — all are for substance, agreeable to the simplicity and plainness of those times. David was a great soldier, a great scholar, and a great prince, and yet a great husband of his estate; and, Wesley adds, those magistrates who would have their subjects industrious must themselves be examples of application to business.

The witnesses gather the whole roll of stewards into a single impression. David's wealth was that of a settled king — treasures on Zion, grain and wine and oil in the country, herds on the coast and in the valleys, camels and she-asses and flocks on the eastern marches — and every part of it lay under a named and responsible ruler, chosen often for his fitness to the charge, as the Ishmaelite to the camels and the Hagerite to the flocks. Ellicott's philology, tracing "substance" back to the movable goods of the nomad, and Wesley's reflection on an administration wholly bent to husbandry rather than to show, meet in one conclusion: that the greatness of David appeared not least in the plain, careful ordering of his estate, where nothing was kept for pomp and nothing left without its overseer.
32:
The record passes at last to the king's inner circle of advisers, and the witnesses read the opening names as a portrait of wisdom near the throne. Keil and Delitzsch note that this catalogue of David's councillors is a companion to the earlier lists of his public officers (1 Chronicles 18:15; 2 Samuel 8:15), yet names in part other men and other duties, so that it enumerates not the chief officers of state but the king's first councillors — his senate, as it were. At its head stands Jonathan, called the "uncle" of David, a counsellor, a wise man, and a scribe; and beside him Jehiel the son of Hachmoni, who was "with the king's sons."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley glosses the two offices. To be a "scribe," he says, means either one learned in the laws of God — which were also the laws of the land — or else the king's secretary; the word will bear both senses, and either befits a trusted counsellor. And Jehiel's charge, to be "with the king's sons," Wesley takes to mean that he was their tutor or governor, set over the education of the princes. So the verse names two kinds of wisdom about the throne: the counsel of a learned kinsman at the king's side, and the careful nurture of the king's children under a chosen guardian.

The witnesses, of different schools, agree on the substance and its worth. Keil's placing of the list — councillors rather than officers of state, a senate of trusted men — frames Wesley's reading of the particular offices, and together they show David surrounding himself and his house with wisdom. His own counsellor was a wise man and a scribe, learned in the law; his sons were committed to a governor for their training. The compiler, having recorded the captains of the army and the stewards of the estate, will not omit the men who advised the king and taught his heirs, for the ordering of a realm reaches to its counsels and its schoolrooms no less than to its fields and its musters. The verse thus opens the last movement of the chapter, in which the wisdom that guides the kingdom is named alongside the strength that guards it and the wealth that sustains it — Jonathan the wise scribe and Jehiel the tutor of princes taking their honoured place in the register of David's reign.
33:
Two famous names from the story of Absalom's revolt stand among David's counsellors, and the witnesses read them with that history in view. Keil and Delitzsch identify Ahithophel, here called "the king's counsellor," with David's confidential adviser whose counsel was esteemed as an oracle, but who took his own life when Absalom, in his conspiracy, would not follow it (2 Samuel 16:23; 2 Samuel 17:23). Hushai the Archite, styled "the king's companion," they identify likewise as the friend and adviser who feigned to serve Absalom and so contrived to defeat the counsel of Ahithophel (2 Samuel 15:37; 2 Samuel 16:16).

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley weighs the two titles and draws from them a pointed contrast. A "counsellor" is the person whose counsel in matters of state the king most prized and followed; a "companion," or friend, is the one whom he trusted with his secrets and whose conversation was most pleasant and acceptable to him. And then Wesley sets the two men side by side with a moralist's eye: "A cunning man was his counsellor, but an honest man was his friend." Ahithophel, for all his oracular wisdom, proved a traitor; Hushai, the king's companion, proved faithful in the hour of danger.

The witnesses thus let the bare list of offices open upon the deepest drama of David's reign. The names of Ahithophel and Hushai cannot be read, they show, without remembering the revolt in which the one's craft turned against the king and the other's friendship saved him. Keil's careful cross-references keep that story in view; Wesley's antithesis distils its lesson — that cunning at the council-table is a poorer thing than honesty at the king's side, and that the trusted friend outlasted the admired adviser. The compiler names both simply as members of David's inner circle, the counsellor and the companion; but the witnesses, reading the register against the histories, find in the pairing a quiet commentary on where a king's true safety lies. It lay not in the celebrated shrewdness of Ahithophel, which failed and destroyed itself, but in the plain fidelity of Hushai, the companion whose loyalty God used to overthrow the wiser man's counsel and preserve the throne.
34:
The chapter closes with the succession of counsellors after Ahithophel and with the general of the host, and the witnesses read the ending both soberly and, in one case, with a flourish of tradition. Keil and Delitzsch explain that "after Ahithophel" means after his death: the place of confidential adviser passed to Jehoiada the son of Benaiah — named, they think, after his grandfather, since sons often bore the grandfather's name — and to Abiathar, without doubt the high priest of that name of the family of Ithamar; while the command of the king's army remained with Joab (1 Chronicles 18:15). John Wesley adds only that "after Ahithophel" marks these as David's chief counsellors once Ahithophel was gone.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke enriches the verse from the Targum, which turns the bare list into a little scene of a nation going to war: they first asked counsel of Ahithophel; after him they inquired by Urim and Thummim of Jehoiada the son of Benaiah, prince of the Sanhedrin and chief of the priesthood, and of Abiathar the high priest; and having so inquired, they went out to battle, well armed, with Joab, the general of the king's troops, leading them on. The embellishment is late and fanciful — a Sanhedrin and an oracle read back into David's day — but it preserves a memory of the true order the verse records, counsel first and command after.

Clarke then draws from the whole roster the moral that crowns the chapter. It is worthy of remark, he says, that Obil the Ishmaelite, an Arab, was set over the camels, the beast of Arabia, and Jaziz the Hagerite, of a people of shepherds, over the flocks: nothing went by favour; each man was appointed to the office for which he was best qualified, and so men of worth were encouraged and the public service effectually promoted. The witnesses end, then, on a note of order and desert. Keil fixes the succession of David's advisers and keeps Joab at the head of the host; Wesley marks the sequence of counsellors; and Clarke, looking back over the camels and the flocks, the counsellors and the captain, finds in the whole a kingdom governed by fitness rather than favour — the fittest keeper for every beast, the wisest head for every counsel, and over all the tried commander Joab, so that the record of David's civil and military house closes as an image of a realm ordered throughout by wisdom and by worth.
